summary: The app writes the official EnableMDNS = 0 policy surface under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient. Microsoft documents that disabled or not configured returns the DNS client to locally configured settings rather than guaranteeing a hard mDNS-off state. This is therefore a naming and scope problem, not a wrong-key problem: the current 'Disable mDNS' UX overstates what the documented policy-disabled state guarantees.

setting.name: mDNS Policy Scope Mapping
setting.casual_explanation: mDNS is a local-network name resolution protocol used by some discovery and service-advertising workflows. The current app uses the documented mDNS policy value, but Microsoft's own help text says the disabled state returns Windows to local settings instead of guaranteeing that mDNS is fully off.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable mDNS when they want less local discovery traffic or when they are hardening or debugging name-resolution behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: The feature area and policy surface are real, but the current app overstates the effect. Microsoft describes the disabled side of this policy as local-settings behavior, not as a guaranteed hard-disable of mDNS.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents EnableMDNS under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0. The help text says enabling the policy makes the DNS client use mDNS, while disabling or not configuring it returns to locally configured settings. In this research pass, the app was confirmed to write the correct mDNS policy value name (EnableMDNS), not the separate LLMNR policy value name (EnableMulticast). No corresponding Microsoft Learn ADMX_DnsClient page entry or local PolicyManager default node for DNS_MDNS was found in this pass, so the strongest source remains the local official ADMX/ADML pair.

allowed_values.label: Local settings
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses locally configured mDNS behavior because the policy is not configured.

allowed_values.label: Policy disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled, which Microsoft describes as returning to locally configured settings rather than publishing a hard off state.

allowed_values.label: Policy enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The DNS client uses mDNS because the policy is enabled.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes the official mDNS policy surface: EnableMDNS = 0 under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient. The mismatch is semantic rather than path-based: Microsoft's policy help text describes value 0 as policy-disabled or local-settings behavior, so the current 'Disable mDNS' wording overstates the documented effect.

windows_defaults.label: Policy not configured
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 1703 and later policy surface documented by Microsoft

states.rationale: Microsoft says the DNS client uses locally configured settings when the policy is not configured.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Anyone who wants a Microsoft-backed baseline
avoid_for: Admins who already have a separate validated method for controlling mDNS

states.rationale: Do not publish the disabled policy state as a hard-disable setting.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to stay inside the documented Windows baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are comparing the current app behavior for research purposes.
tradeoffs: Does not force a feature-off stance.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile
recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting what the current product writes.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a published hard-disable mapping.
tradeoffs: Does not meet the validation bar for a trustworthy disable record.

evidence.title: Microsoft administrative template for mDNS
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents EnableMDNS under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0.

evidence.title: Microsoft help text for mDNS policy
evidence.summary: Microsoft says enabling the policy makes the DNS client use mDNS, while disabling or not configuring the policy returns to locally configured settings.

evidence.title: Current network tweak provider
evidence.summary: The current app writes EnableMDNS = 0 for the disable-mDNS tweak.

decision.why: The app writes the documented mDNS policy path and value name, but Microsoft's documented semantics for value 0 are narrower than the current UX claim. This is a naming and scope issue: the product presents policy-disabled local-settings behavior as a direct mDNS hard-disable.
blocking_issues: Naming mismatch: the current app describes the official policy-disabled local-settings behavior (EnableMDNS = 0) as a direct mDNS disable.
No stronger Microsoft-documented hard-disable surface for mDNS was captured in this pass.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="DNS_MDNS" class="Machine" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient" valueName="EnableMDNS">; DnsClient.adml: Specifies if the DNS client will perform name resolution over mDNS. If you enable this policy, the DNS client will use mDNS protocol.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX and ADML files document the exact EnableMDNS policy surface. The key finding is semantic: value 0 is documented as policy-disabled/local-settings behavior, not as a guaranteed hard-disable state. In the same pass, the app was also confirmed to use EnableMDNS rather than the separate LLMNR value EnableMulticast.
